4. `PATCH /tickets/{id}` to update `subject`, `description`, `priority`, or `status`.
5. `DELETE /tickets/{id}` to delete a ticket.
6. `GET /metrics` returning counts by status and priority plus average age of open tickets in seconds.

Engineering requirements:
- Read/write a `tickets.json` file safely enough for sequential requests.
- Return JSON for all responses, including errors.
- Include CORS headers for local browser testing.
- Handle invalid JSON, unknown routes, unsupported methods, and missing IDs.
- Start on `127.0.0.1:8080` by default.

Output only the complete JavaScript code. Do not include explanations.

Create a single-file frontend app named `index.html`. Do not use external libraries, CDNs, images, or build tools. Keep the total code under 1,000 lines.

Task: build an accessible “meeting notes board” for a small team.

Functional requirements:
1. Users can add a note with title, owner, meeting date, tags, and body.
2. Notes appear as cards and can be filtered by owner, tag, and free-text search.
3. A note can be marked as “Action Required,” “Decision,” or “Reference.”
4. Users can edit and delete notes.
5. Persist all data in `localStorage`.
6. Provide a compact statistics strip: total notes, action-required count, unique owners, and most common tag.
7. Responsive layout: two-column card grid on wide screens, single column on narrow screens.
8. Basic accessibility: labels for inputs, keyboard-operable controls, visible focus states.

Output only the complete `index.html` content, including HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. Do not include explanatory text.

Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary for modern business readers.

Output requirements:
1. A 100-word executive summary.
2. Five principles of effective business writing implied by the author.
3. A table with two columns: “Original-era advice” and “Modern equivalent.” Include at least five rows.
4. Identify any advice that may be dated and explain how to update it without losing the underlying principle.
5. Do not add claims that are not grounded in the passage.

素材来源：The Book of Business Etiquette, chapter “The Business of Writing” longer excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
Half the business letters which are written should never be written at all, and of the other half so many are incomplete or incoherent that a transaction which could be finished and filed away in two letters frequently requires six or eight.

A good letter is the result of clear thinking and careful planning. In the case of the sales-letter it sometimes takes several weeks to write one, but for ordinary correspondence a few minutes is usually all that is necessary. The length of time does not matter--it is the sort of letter which is produced at the end of it.

Books of commercial correspondence give a number of rules and standards by which a letter can be measured. But all rules of thumb are dangerous, and there are only two items which are essential. The others are valuable only as they contribute to them. The letter must succeed in getting its idea across and it must build up good will for its firm. And the best one is the one which accomplishes this most courteously and most completely in the briefest space of time (and paper).

There should be a reason back of every letter if it is only to say "Thank you" to a customer. Too much of our national energy goes up in waste effort, in aimless advertising, worthless salesmanship, ineffective letter writing, and in a thousand and one other ways. A lot of it is hammered out on the typewriters transcribing perfectly useless letters to paper which might really be worth something if it were given over to a different purpose.

A good letter never attracts the mind of the reader to itself as a thing apart from its contents. Last year a publishing house sent out a hundred test letters advertising one of their books. Three answers came back, none of them ordering the book, but all three praising the letter. One was from a teacher of commercial English who declared that he was going to use it as a model in his classes, and the other two congratulated the firm on having so excellent a correspondent. The physical make-up of the letter was attractive, it was written by a college graduate and couched in clear, correct, and colorful English. And yet it was no good. No _letter and no advertisement is any good which calls attention to itself instead of the message it is trying to deliver_.

There is not much room for individuality in the make-up of a letter. Custom has standardized it, and startling variations from the conventional format indicates freakishness rather than originality. They are like that astonishing gentleman who walks up Fifth Avenue on the coldest mornings in the year, bareheaded, coatless, sockless, clad in white flannels and tennis slippers. He attracts attention, but he makes us shiver.

Plain white paper of good quality is always in good taste. Certain dull-tinted papers are not bad, but gaudy colors, flashy designs, and ornate letter heads are taboo in all high types of business. Simple headings giving explicit and useful information are best. The name and address of the firm (and "New York" or "Chicago" is not sufficient in spite of the fact that a good many places go into no more detail than this), the cable address if it has one, the telephone number and the trademark if it is an inconspicuous one (there is a difference between _conspicuous_ and _distinctive_) are all that any business house needs.

Hotels are often pictured on their own stationery in a way that is anything but modest, but there is a very good reason for it. The first thing most people want to know about a hotel is what sort of looking place it is. All right, here you are. Some factories, especially those that are proud of their appearance, carry their own picture on their stationery. There is nothing to say against it, but one of the most beautiful factories in America has on its letter head only the name of the firm, the address, and a small trademark engraved in black. Sometimes a picture, in a sales letter, for instance, supplements the written matter in a most effective way. And whenever any kind of device is really helpful it should by all means be used, subject only to the limits of good taste.

It is more practical in business to use standard size envelopes. If window envelopes are used the window should be clear, the paper white or nearly so, and the typewritten address a good honest black. The enclosure should fit snugly and should be placed so that the address is in plain view without having to be jiggled around in the envelope first. A letter passes through the hands of several postal clerks before it reaches the person to whom it is addressed, and if each one of them has to stop to play with it awhile an appreciable amount of time is lost, not to mention the strain it puts on their respective tempers. The paper of which an envelope is made should always be opaque enough to conceal the contents of the letter.

Practically all business letters are typewritten. Occasionally a "Help Wanted" advertisement requests that the answer be in the applicant's own handwriting, but even this is rare. In most places the typing is taken care of by girls who have been trained for the purpose, but most young girls just entering business are highly irresponsible, and it is necessary for the men and women who dictate the letters to know what constitutes a pleasing make-up so that they can point out the flaws and give suggestions for doing away with them.

The letter should be arranged symmetrically on the page with ample margins all around. Nothing but experience in copying her own notes will teach a stenographer to estimate them correctly so that she will not have to rewrite badly placed letters. It is a little point, but an important one.

Each subject considered in a letter should be treated in a separate paragraph, and each paragraph should be set off from the others by a wider space than that between the lines, double space between the paragraphs when there is single space between the lines, triple space between the paragraphs when there is a double space between the lines, and so on.

A business letter should handle only one subject. Two letters should be dispatched if two subjects are to be covered. This enables the house receiving the letter to file it so that it can be found when it is needed.

When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin "Dear Mr. Brown" or "My dear Mr. Brown" than "Dear Sir" or "My dear Sir." "Gentlemen" or "Ladies" is sometime used in salutation when a letter is addressed to a group. "Dear Friend" is permissible in general letters sent out to persons of both sexes. Honorary titles should be used in the address when they take the place of "Mr.," such titles as Reverend, Doctor, Honorable (abbreviated to Rev., Dr., Hon.,) and the like. Titles should not be dropped except in the case of personal letters.

Special care should be taken with the outside address. State abbreviations should be used sparingly when there is a chance of confusion as in the case of Ga., Va., La., and Pa. "City" is not sufficient and should never be used. Nor should the name of the state ever be omitted even when the letter is addressed to some other point in the same state, as from New York to Brooklyn. And postage should be complete. A letter on which there is two cents due has placed itself under a pretty severe handicap before it is opened.

It is astonishing how many letters go out every day unsigned, lacking enclosures, carrying the wrong addresses, bearing insufficient postage, and showing other evidences of carelessness and thoughtlessness. In a town in New England last year one of the specialty shops received at Christmas time twenty different lots of money--money orders, stamps, and cash--by mail, not one of which bore the slightest clue to the identity of the sender. Countless times during the year this happens in every mail order house.

message.content:
Here is a structured English summary of the passage for modern business readers.

### Executive Summary
The passage argues that business writing suffers from volume over value: half of all letters are unnecessary, and many others are so poorly organized that they multiply correspondence needlessly. Effective writing demands clear thinking and planning, not just rule-following. The two essential goals are getting the idea across and building goodwill, achieved concisely and courteously. A letter fails if readers notice the writing itself rather than its message. Format should be conventional to signal professionalism, not freakishness. Practical details—appropriate stationery, clear addressing, one subject per letter—prevent confusion and wasted effort. Ultimately, every letter must have a purpose, even if only to say thank you.

### Five Principles of Effective Business Writing
1.  **Every message must have a clear, valid reason for being sent** (not wasted effort for its own sake).
2.  **The medium must not overshadow the message** (style should serve content, not compete with it).
3.  **Clarity and completeness come from planning**, not from gimmicks or elaborate language.
4.  **Goodwill is a deliberate goal**, achieved through courtesy, accuracy, and respect for the reader’s time.
5.  **Professionalism is conveyed through consistent, appropriate formatting**, not unusual or flashy design.

### Original-Era Advice vs. Modern Equivalent

| Original-era advice | Modern equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Use plain white paper of good quality; avoid gaudy colors and flashy designs. | Use clean, professional email templates and standard fonts (e.g., Arial, Calibri); avoid distracting backgrounds or excessive formatting. |
| Use standard-size envelopes; ensure window envelopes display the address clearly without jiggling. | Use clear, descriptive subject lines in emails; ensure attachments are correctly named and formatted for easy access. |
| Handle only one subject per letter; send two letters if two subjects are covered. | Send one focused email per topic; use separate threads for distinct projects or issues to aid search and filing. |
| Begin “Dear Mr. Brown” rather than “Dear Sir”; use “Gentlemen” or “Ladies” for groups. | Address the recipient by name (e.g., “Hi Sarah” or “Dear Dr. Chen”); use “Dear Team” or “Hello everyone” for groups. |
| Ensure outside address is complete, including state, and that postage is fully paid. | Ensure “To” field is accurate, use CC/BCC appropriately, and check that attachments are included before sending. |

### Dated Advice and How to Update It

**Dated advice:** “When a letter is addressed to an individual it is better to begin ‘Dear Mr. Brown’ or ‘My dear Mr. Brown’ than ‘Dear Sir’.” The original principle is to treat the recipient as a person rather than an anonymous title.

**How to update it without losing the principle:** In modern business, always use the recipient’s name when known (e.g., “Hello Jordan” or “Dear Dr. Patel”). In emails to groups, avoid the gendered “Gentlemen” or “Ladies”; use “Dear Team,” “Hello everyone,” or “Hi colleagues.” This preserves the courtesy and personalization of the original advice while respecting contemporary norms around inclusivity and formality.

messages.content:
Read the public-domain passage below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A 120-word maximum plot summary.
2. A bullet list of Mary’s character traits shown in the passage, each supported by a concrete detail.
3. A short paragraph explaining how the setting creates emotional distance or isolation.
4. Three discussion questions suitable for ages 10–12.
5. Do not add plot information from outside the passage.

素材来源：Frances Hodgson Burnett, The Secret Garden, opening excerpt
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/113
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
When Mary Lennox was sent to Misselthwaite Manor to live with her uncle everybody said she was the most disagreeable-looking child ever seen. It was true, too. She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression. Her hair was yellow, and her face was yellow because she had been born in India and had always been ill in one way or another. Her father had held a position under the English Government and had always been busy and ill himself, and her mother had been a great beauty who cared only to go to parties and amuse herself with gay people. She had not wanted a little girl at all, and when Mary was born she handed her over to the care of an Ayah, who was made to understand that if she wished to please the Mem Sahib she must keep the child out of sight as much as possible. So when she was a sickly, fretful, ugly little baby she was kept out of the way, and when she became a sickly, fretful, toddling thing she was kept out of the way also. She never remembered seeing familiarly anything but the dark faces of her Ayah and the other native servants, and as they always obeyed her and gave her her own way in everything, because the Mem Sahib would be angry if she was disturbed by her crying, by the time she was six years old she was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived. The young English governess who came to teach her to read and write disliked her so much that she gave up her place in three months, and when other governesses came to try to fill it they always went away in a shorter time than the first one. So if Mary had not chosen to really want to know how to read books she would never have learned her letters at all.

One frightfully hot morning, when she was about nine years old, she awakened feeling very cross, and she became crosser still when she saw that the servant who stood by her bedside was not her Ayah.

“Why did you come?” she said to the strange woman. “I will not let you stay. Send my Ayah to me.”

The woman looked frightened, but she only stammered that the Ayah could not come and when Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her, she looked only more frightened and repeated that it was not possible for the Ayah to come to Missie Sahib.

There was something mysterious in the air that morning. Nothing was done in its regular order and several of the native servants seemed missing, while those whom Mary saw slunk or hurried about with ashy and scared faces. But no one would tell her anything and her Ayah did not come. She was actually left alone as the morning went on, and at last she wandered out into the garden and began to play by herself under a tree near the veranda. She pretended that she was making a flower-bed, and she stuck big scarlet hibiscus blossoms into little heaps of earth, all the time growing more and more angry and muttering to herself the things she would say and the names she would call Saidie when she returned.

“Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” she said, because to call a native a pig is the worst insult of all.

She was grinding her teeth and saying this over and over again when she heard her mother come out on the veranda with someone. She was with a fair young man and they stood talking together in low strange voices. Mary knew the fair young man who looked like a boy. She had heard that he was a very young officer who had just come from England. The child stared at him, but she stared most at her mother. She always did this when she had a chance to see her, because the Mem Sahib—Mary used to call her that oftener than anything else—was such a tall, slim, pretty person and wore such lovely clothes. Her hair was like curly silk and she had a delicate little nose which seemed to be disdaining things, and she had large laughing eyes. All her clothes were thin and floating, and Mary said they were “full of lace.” They looked fuller of lace than ever this morning, but her eyes were not laughing at all. They were large and scared and lifted imploringly to the fair boy officer’s face.

“Is it so very bad? Oh, is it?” Mary heard her say.

“Awfully,” the young man answered in a trembling voice. “Awfully, Mrs. Lennox. You ought to have gone to the hills two weeks ago.”

The Mem Sahib wrung her hands.

“Oh, I know I ought!” she cried. “I only stayed to go to that silly dinner party. What a fool I was!”

At that very moment such a loud sound of wailing broke out from the servants’ quarters that she clutched the young man’s arm, and Mary stood shivering from head to foot. The wailing grew wilder and wilder. “What is it? What is it?” Mrs. Lennox gasped.

“Someone has died,” answered the boy officer. “You did not say it had broken out among your servants.”

“I did not know!” the Mem Sahib cried. “Come with me! Come with me!” and she turned and ran into the house.

After that appalling things happened, and the mysteriousness of the morning was explained to Mary. The cholera had broken out in its most fatal form and people were dying like flies. The Ayah had been taken ill in the night, and it was because she had just died that the servants had wailed in the huts. Before the next day three other servants were dead and others had run away in terror. There was panic on every side, and dying people in all the bungalows.

During the confusion and bewilderment of the second day Mary hid herself in the nursery and was forgotten by everyone. Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her, and strange things happened of which she knew nothing. Mary alternately cried and slept through the hours. She only knew that people were ill and that she heard mysterious and frightening sounds. Once she crept into the dining-room and found it empty, though a partly finished meal was on the table and chairs and plates looked as if they had been hastily pushed back when the diners rose suddenly for some reason. The child ate some fruit and biscuits, and being thirsty she drank a glass of wine which stood nearly filled. It was sweet, and she did not know how strong it was. Very soon it made her intensely drowsy, and she went back to her nursery and shut herself in again, frightened by cries she heard in the huts and by the hurrying sound of feet. The wine made her so sleepy that she could scarcely keep her eyes open and she lay down on her bed and knew nothing more for a long time.

message.content:
### Plot Summary (120 words max)
Mary Lennox, a sickly, sour-faced child, is sent to live with her uncle at Misselthwaite Manor after a cholera outbreak in India kills her parents and servants. Raised ignored by her beautiful, party-loving mother and indulged by native servants, Mary became tyrannical and selfish. One hot morning, her Ayah is absent; Mary throws a tantrum, but servants look scared and avoid her. She overhears her mother speaking anxiously to a young officer about the cholera. Suddenly, wailing erupts—the Ayah has died. Panic spreads; servants flee or die. Mary hides in the nursery, forgotten. She eats leftover food, drinks wine, and falls into a deep sleep, alone and unnoticed.

### Mary’s Character Traits with Supporting Details
- **Disagreeable appearance**: “She had a little thin face and a little thin body, thin light hair and a sour expression.”
- **Tyrannical**: “When Mary threw herself into a passion and beat and kicked her [the servant].”
- **Selfish**: “She was as tyrannical and selfish a little pig as ever lived.”
- **Fretful**: She awakens “feeling very cross” and becomes crosser when her Ayah is absent.
- **Verbally cruel**: She mutters “Pig! Pig! Daughter of Pigs!” as an insult.
- **Ignored/unwanted**: “Nobody thought of her, nobody wanted her” during the cholera crisis.

### Setting’s Emotional Distance or Isolation
The passage’s setting—first in India, then at Misselthwaite Manor—creates isolation through neglect. Mary’s mother hands her to an Ayah to keep “out of sight,” and later, during the cholera outbreak, Mary is “forgotten by everyone.” Servants slink away with “ashy and scared faces,” refusing to explain, and she hides alone in the nursery. The empty dining room, with hastily abandoned chairs, underscores her abandonment. No one seeks her, and she falls asleep unnoticed, reinforcing emotional distance.

### Discussion Questions (Ages 10–12)
1. Why do you think Mary behaves so meanly to the servant who wakes her? What does this tell you about how she was raised?
2. How would you feel if, like Mary, you were forgotten by everyone during a scary emergency? What would you do?
3. The story says Mary’s mother “did not want a little girl at all.” How might this have affected Mary’s personality and actions?

messages.content:
Read the public-domain science explainer below and produce a structured English summary.

Output requirements:
1. A concise 100-word overview.
2. A concept map in text form using arrows, showing how water moves among major pools.
3. A list of at least eight key terms with one-sentence definitions based only on the passage.
4. A section titled “Human influence” summarizing how human actions affect water movement, storage, or availability.
5. Do not introduce outside climate, policy, or regional examples.

素材来源：U.S. Geological Survey Water Science School: Water cycle
来源链接：https://www.usgs.gov/water-science-school/water-cycle
版权/授权说明：USGS-authored information is considered U.S. public domain.

【待处理素材】
The water cycle describes where water is on Earth and how it moves. Human water use, land use, and climate change all impact the water cycle. By understanding these impacts, we can work toward using water sustainably.

Viewed from space, the most striking feature of our planet is the water. In both liquid and frozen form, it covers 75% of the Earth's surface. It fills the sky with clouds. Water is practically everywhere on Earth, from inside the planet's rocky crust to inside the cells of the human body. What's important to keep in mind is that all of this water is in constant motion across our planet.

Water is stored in the atmosphere, on the land surface, and below the ground. It can be a liquid, a solid, or a gas. Liquid water can be fresh or saline. Water moves between the places it is stored. Water moves at large scales, through watersheds, the atmosphere, and below the Earth's surface. Water moves at very small scales too. It is in us, plants, and other organisms.

Oceans store 96% of all water on Earth. Ocean water is saline, meaning it’s salty. On land, saline water is stored in saline lakes. The rest of the water on Earth is fresh water. Fresh water is stored in liquid form in freshwater lakes, artificial reservoirs, rivers, and wetlands. Water is stored in solid, frozen form in ice sheets and glaciers, and in snowpack at high elevations or near Earth's poles.

As it moves, water can change form between liquid, solid, and gas. Circulation mixes water in the oceans and transports water vapor in the atmosphere. Water moves between the atmosphere and the surface through evaporation, evapotranspiration, and precipitation. Water moves across the surface through snowmelt, runoff, and streamflow. Water moves into the ground through infiltration and groundwater recharge. Underground, groundwater flows within aquifers. Groundwater can return to the surface through natural discharge into rivers, the ocean, and from springs.

Water moves naturally and because of human actions. Energy from the sun and the force of gravity drive the continual movement of water between pools. The sun’s energy causes liquid water to evaporate into water vapor. Evapotranspiration is the main way water moves into the atmosphere from the land surface and oceans. Gravity causes water to flow downward on land. It causes rain, snow, and hail to fall from clouds.

Humans and other organisms rely on water for life. The amount of water that is available depends on how much water there is in each pool, when and how fast water moves through the water cycle, and how clean the water is. By understanding human impacts on the water cycle, we can work toward using water sustainably.

message.content:
### Concise 100-word Overview
The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on Earth among atmospheric, surface, and underground pools. Oceans hold 96% of Earth’s water; the rest is fresh or saline water stored in lakes, rivers, ice, groundwater, and the atmosphere. Solar energy and gravity drive water’s movement through evaporation, evapotranspiration, precipitation, runoff, infiltration, and groundwater flow. Water changes between liquid, solid, and gas forms as it circulates. Human water use, land use, and climate change affect water storage, movement, and availability. Understanding these impacts helps promote sustainable water use.

### Concept Map (Text Form)
```
Atmosphere
   ↓ precipitation (rain, snow, hail)
   ↓
Land Surface (lakes, rivers, wetlands, snowpack, glaciers)
   ↓ runoff, streamflow, snowmelt
   ↓
Oceans (saline) ←—— natural discharge from rivers/groundwater
   ↑ evaporation, evapotranspiration
   ↓ infiltration
Groundwater (aquifers) —→ natural discharge into rivers, ocean, springs
      ↑ groundwater recharge
   ←—— upward flow (evapotranspiration from plants/soil)
```

### Key Terms (with One-Sentence Definitions)
1. **Water cycle**: The continuous movement of water on Earth among atmospheric, surface, and underground storage pools.
2. **Evaporation**: The process by which solar energy turns liquid water into water vapor.
3. **Evapotranspiration**: The combined movement of water into the atmosphere from land surfaces and oceans.
4. **Precipitation**: Rain, snow, or hail that falls from clouds due to gravity.
5. **Runoff**: Water that flows across the land surface toward streams or other water bodies.
6. **Infiltration**: The movement of water from the land surface into the ground.
7. **Groundwater recharge**: The process by which water enters underground aquifers.
8. **Aquifer**: An underground layer where groundwater flows and is stored.
9. **Natural discharge**: The return of groundwater to the surface through rivers, oceans, or springs.

### Human Influence
Human actions affect the water cycle primarily through water use, land use, and climate change. Water extraction for drinking, agriculture, and industry reduces storage in surface and groundwater pools. Land-use changes (e.g., urbanization, deforestation) alter infiltration rates, runoff patterns, and evapotranspiration. Climate change—driven by human emissions—modifies precipitation timing, intensity, and form, as well as evaporation rates. These impacts change how much water is available in each pool, how fast water moves through the cycle, and the quality of water resources.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word executive update email from the COO of a fictional direct-to-consumer home goods company to all employees. Context: a warehouse management system migration has reduced shipping speed for one week, and customer support volume is up.

Requirements:
- Tone: accountable, calm, specific, and respectful of frontline teams.
- Explain what happened without blaming a team or vendor.
- Include current customer impact, what is being done today, what will change next week, and how teams should escalate urgent issues.
- Include a concise manager talking-points section with 4 bullets.
- Avoid clichés, excessive apology, legal language, or promises that cannot be guaranteed.

message.content:
**Subject:** Update on Warehouse Systems Migration & Current Shipping Delays  

**Team,**  

I’m writing to give you a direct, honest update on a situation that has affected our shipping speed over the past week and increased volume for our customer support team. This is a challenging moment for operations, but I want to be clear: I own the outcome, and I am grateful for the steady, professional way each of you has handled the disruption.  

**What happened**  

Last weekend, we completed a planned migration of our warehouse management system (WMS) to a new platform. This was a necessary upgrade to support our growth, improve inventory accuracy, and enable faster fulfillment in the long term. However, the transition introduced unexpected data syncing delays between the new WMS and our shipping carrier integrations.  

The root cause was a mismatch in how the new system handles order-to-carrier handoff timing—specifically, batch release intervals that were too aggressive for the initial load. This caused orders to stall in a “pending carrier pickup” status for hours longer than expected. Our engineering team and warehouse operations leads identified the issue on day two, but resolving it required coordinated changes to both the WMS configuration and carrier API settings, which took longer than anticipated due to weekend staffing gaps.  

I want to be clear: this was a planning miss on my part. We tested the migration in staging, but we did not simulate real-world peak order flow with the exact carrier configurations that went live. No single team or vendor is at fault—this was a systemic oversight in our rollout approach, and I take responsibility for not building in a slower ramp-up period.  

**Current impact on customers and teams**  

- **Shipping speed:** For the past seven days, standard orders have been delayed by an average of 1.8 business days compared to our usual next-day dispatch. Approximately 12% of orders placed last week are still in “processing” status beyond our 24-hour promise.  
- **Customer support volume:** Our support team has seen a 40% increase in contacts, primarily from customers asking for order status updates. Response time has stretched from our usual <4 hours to an average of 8 hours during peak periods.  
- **Warehouse team:** Pick and pack teams have worked through system lag and manual workarounds to keep orders moving. They deserve significant recognition for maintaining safety and accuracy under pressure.  

**What we are doing today**  

1. **Order prioritization:** We have implemented a manual override process to release any order older than 48 hours in “pending” status. This is being run hourly by our warehouse leads.  
2. **Carrier escalation:** Our logistics coordinator is in direct contact with our primary carriers to secure priority pickup slots for delayed orders.  
3. **Support staffing:** We have temporarily reassigned four people from our returns and logistics teams to frontline support to reduce wait times. Overtime is approved at 1.5x for any support teammate working extended hours this week.  
4. **Customer communication:** We are sending proactive email updates to every customer whose order has not yet shipped—including a revised estimated delivery date based on the actual warehouse status. No overpromises; we are giving conservative timelines.  

**What will change next week**  

- **System fix deployed:** Our engineering team has deployed a fix that adjusts the order-to-carrier sync interval from every 15 minutes to every 5 minutes, with a manual override tolerance for high-volume hours. We will monitor this closely Monday through Wednesday.  
- **Rollback plan formalized:** If we see any order backlog exceeding 12 hours on Monday, we will revert to the legacy WMS for a 48-hour period while we re-validate the new configuration with a more gradual rollout. This fallback is already tested.  
- **Customer promise adjustment:** We are temporarily updating our website checkout page to show “ships within 2 business days” instead of “ships tomorrow” until we have three consecutive days of on-time dispatch ≥95%. We will not restore the original promise until the data confirms stability.  
- **Support staffing plan:** We will maintain the four-person temporary support allocation through the end of next week and evaluate whether additional headcount is needed.  

**Escalation path for urgent issues**  

If you encounter a situation that requires immediate attention (e.g., a customer order that is late for a known life event like a move or celebration, or a warehouse safety concern caused by system workarounds), please escalate directly to:  

- **For operational or logistics issues:** Your warehouse lead → then to **Sarah L., Director of Fulfillment** (sarah.l@company.com or Slack @sarah-ops)  
- **For customer support escalation:** Your team lead → then to **Marcus T., Head of Customer Experience** (marcus.t@company.com or Slack @marcus-cx)  
- **For any issue that affects team safety or morale:** My chief of staff, **David R.** (david.r@company.com), will respond within one hour during business hours.  

I will host a 15-minute stand-up on **Monday at 9:00 AM ET** in the #ops-updates Slack channel for any team member who wants to ask questions directly.  

**Manager talking points for your team conversations**  

If your team asks for context, here is a concise set of messages you can use:  

- **The migration was necessary for growth, but we moved too fast.** The system change was the right decision long-term, but we did not test with real-world carrier conditions. I (the COO) own the rollout plan, not any individual team or vendor.  
- **Delays are being corrected hourly.** We have a manual override process running now to release stuck orders. We expect shipping speed to return to normal by mid-next week.  
- **Customer support volume is high, but we are responding honestly.** Proactive emails are going out with realistic delivery dates. No one is being asked to promise more than we can deliver.  
- **Escalate if something feels urgent or unsafe.** We have clear processes for both operational and people concerns. Use the Slack channels above—do not hesitate to flag issues.  

Thank you for holding the line this week. I know our frontline teams—warehouse, customer support, and logistics—have carried the heaviest load, and I respect the patience and professionalism you’ve shown. We will get through this, and we will be stronger because of how we handled it.  

With accountability,  

**[Your Name]**  
COO | [Company Name]

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word story for children ages 7–10. The main characters are a careful snail who keeps a map of every puddle in the garden and a young sparrow who wants to fly before she has learned how to land. The story should take place after a summer rain.

Requirements:
- Complete story arc with a gentle problem and resolution.
- Warm, concrete language suitable for children.
- Include one simple nature concept, such as how puddles dry or how birds use wind, explained naturally through the story.
- Theme: patience can be a kind of bravery.
- No violence, death, horror, or adult romance.

message.content:
Here is a story for children ages 7–10.

---

### The Sparrow and the Puddle Map

The world after a summer rain is a brand-new place. The air smells like wet earth and green things. Every leaf has a tiny, trembling diamond on it. And for Barnaby the snail, it was the most wonderful time of all.

Barnaby was a careful snail. His shell was the color of caramel, and he carried a tiny, rolled-up leaf under his belly. This was not just any leaf. It was his Puddle Map. He had spent the whole spring drawing it. Each puddle in the garden was marked with a careful wiggly line and a little dot. The Big One under the downspout. The Three Sisters by the stone wall. The Mirror under the oak tree.

Barnaby was out that morning, tracing his map with a dewdrop. He was checking to see if the Big One had grown bigger. Being careful was his kind of brave. It took bravery to inch, inch, inch all the way across a wet, slippery paving stone when a giant human foot might stomp by at any moment.

Just then, a blur of brown feathers bounced onto the branch above him. It was Pip, a young sparrow. Pip was learning to fly, but she had a problem. She only liked the *first* part.

“Whoosh!” she chirped, fluffing her wings. “The wind is pushing today! I can feel it in my feathers!”

Barnaby looked up. “Hello, Pip,” he said slowly. “The wind is strong. Be careful.”

“Careful is boring!” Pip chirped. “I want to fly to the top of the oak tree! I want to fly over the fence! I want to see the *whole* world!”

She hopped to the tip of the branch and wobbled. “Watch this!” She jumped.

Pip tumbled through the air, flapping as fast as she could. She rose a little, then tipped sideways. She flapped harder. She went up and down like a yo-yo. “Wheeee!” she cried. Then she lost the wind completely and dropped into a big, leafy bush with a *thump*.

She popped out, ruffled and grumpy. “My wings just stopped working!”

Barnaby had carefully finished his circle of the Big One. “You need to learn to catch the wind, not just fight it,” he said. “And you need to learn how to use your tail to steer when you land.”

Pip hopped closer. “Landing is the boring part! The last part! I want the *flying* part!”

Barnaby unrolled his Puddle Map. “See these dots? I have to know where the water is. If I don’t, I might get stuck on a dry stone and dry out. I have to be patient, or I’ll get hurt.”

Pip looked at the map. She saw a puddle shaped like a teardrop. “What’s that one?”

“The Mirror,” said Barnaby. “Under the oak tree. It’s the deepest.”

Pip’s eyes went wide. She had an idea. “The deepest! That’s where I should practice my…”

“Your what?” asked Barnaby.

“My… flying! From a high place. Onto a soft, wet place!” she chirped. “That puddle is perfect!”

Barnaby’s heart went *click* with worry. “It’s not a soft, wet place, Pip. It’s water. You could get your wings all wet and heavy. And it’s deep for a snail. Please, be careful.”

But Pip was already hop-skittering down the branch. “I’ll fly over it! You’ll see!”

She flapped to a higher branch. The puddle below looked like a giant, shiny blue eye. Barnaby watched from the edge of the stone wall, his map clutched tight. He wanted to shout, “Don’t do it!” But he knew she had to learn.

Pip took a deep breath. “For the top of the oak tree!” she chirped, and she jumped.

The wind caught her this time. She soared across the garden. It was perfect! For two whole seconds. Then she tried to turn. She remembered Barnaby’s words about the tail. She tried to steer, but she twisted too much. She stopped flying and started falling.

*Splash!*

Pip landed right in the middle of the Mirror. Water flew up in a silver spray. She was soaked. Her wings were heavy. She looked like a drowned mop.

Barnaby moved as fast as a snail can move. “Hold on! Swim to the edge!” he called. Pip paddled with her feet, but her wings were too tired. She was scared.

Then Barnaby had an idea. A very brave, very careful idea.

He crawled onto a flat lily pad floating near the edge. “Grab this!” he said. “I’ll pull you to the shore!”

Pip wobbled over and put her beak on the lily pad. Barnaby leaned all his weight back. His foot slid on the wet pad. He pulled. Nothing happened.

Be patient, he told himself. *This is the brave part.*

He took a deep breath. He didn’t yank. He pulled, slow and steady, *inch* by *inch*. The lily pad moved. *Inch. Inch. Inch.* It was the bravest pulling he had ever done.

Finally, Pip’s feet touched the mud. She flopped onto a warm, dry stone. She shook herself, shivering. “I—I didn’t know how to stop,” she whispered.

Barnaby rested beside her. “Flying is like a story, Pip. The start is exciting. But the ending is just as important. You have to learn how to close the book gently.”

Pip looked at his map. Then she looked at him. “You saved me,” she said. “You were so slow, but you were so strong.”

Barnaby smiled. “Being careful is my speed. And you? You’re brave for trying to fly. But being patient is a different kind of brave. It’s the brave of waiting and learning, not just jumping.”

Pip thought about that. She looked at the sky. The wind was still pushing, gentle and cool. She felt her wings beginning to dry.

“Will you teach me?” she asked quietly. “How to watch the wind? And how to point my tail to land?”

Barnaby rolled up his map. “I can try. But first, let’s watch the puddles dry.” He pointed. “See how the sun is making the Mirror smaller? The water is going back to the sky to make more clouds. By lunchtime, it will be just a damp spot. Everything has a rhythm.”

Pip sat very still and watched the sun slowly drink the water. She watched a tiny bubble pop. She didn’t try to fly. She just watched.

And for the first time, she felt a new kind of brave. It didn’t go *whoosh* like a gust of wind. It went *tick… tock…* like a snail on a stone, slow and steady and sure. It was the brave of the journey, not just the leap. And it felt, Pip decided, almost as good as flying.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word press release in English based only on the fictional facts below. Do not invent funding amounts, celebrity endorsers, political supporters, or external partners. Use a professional, journalistic style with a headline, dateline, lead paragraph, supporting details, at least one direct quote, and a media contact block.

Fictional facts:
- Organization: Harborleaf Botanical Garden.
- Announcement: Launch of a new “Pollinator Path” public education trail.
- Opening date: July 12, 2026.
- Location: The east meadow loop, a 0.8-mile accessible trail.
- Features: native plant demonstration beds, night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, shaded resting points, and a volunteer-led weekend walk.
- Motivation: visitors have asked for more practical guidance on planting for bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds.
- Quote speaker: Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs. Core message: small planting decisions can create connected habitats across neighborhoods.
- Keep the topic nonpolitical and educational.

message.content:
**FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE**

**Harborleaf Botanical Garden Unveils “Pollinator Path” Trail to Inspire Community-Based Habitat Conservation**

**HARBORLEAF, CA – June 10, 2026** – The Harborleaf Botanical Garden will open its new “Pollinator Path” public education trail on July 12, 2026, offering visitors a dedicated, accessible route designed to demonstrate how individual planting choices can support bees, butterflies, moths, and hummingbirds. The 0.8-mile trail, located on the garden’s east meadow loop, will feature native plant demonstration beds, a night-blooming flower station, tactile signage, youth observation cards, and shaded resting points, along with a volunteer-led weekend walk program.

The trail’s development responds to a growing number of visitor inquiries seeking practical, actionable guidance on creating pollinator-friendly spaces. Garden staff observed that many guests leave the main gardens inspired but unsure how to translate botanical beauty into their own yards, balconies, or community plots. The Pollinator Path directly addresses that gap by providing clear, hands-on learning opportunities at every stop.

“We heard repeatedly from visitors that they want to help pollinators, but they don’t know where to start,” said Maya Ortiz, Director of Learning Programs at Harborleaf Botanical Garden. “This trail shows that small planting decisions—choosing a native milkweed over an ornamental, or leaving a patch of bare soil for ground-nesting bees—can create connected habitats that stretch across neighborhoods. It’s not about grand gestures; it’s about informed, consistent choices.”

The trail begins at the east meadow loop entrance, where a welcome kiosk introduces the concept of pollinator corridors. From there, visitors will encounter a series of themed stations. The native plant demonstration beds feature locally adapted species such as California fuchsia, yarrow, and goldenrod, each labeled with information about which pollinators they attract and their bloom cycles. A dedicated “night-blooming flower station” highlights plants like evening primrose and yucca that support moths and bats after dusk.

Accessibility was a central design consideration. The entire 0.8-mile loop uses compacted, stable surfacing suitable for wheelchairs, strollers, and walkers. Tactile signage incorporating raised text, braille, and embossed illustrations allows visitors with visual impairments to identify plants and learn about their pollinator relationships. Benches and shaded resting points are spaced at regular intervals, and the path maintains a gentle grade throughout.

Youth engagement is a priority. At the start of the trail, children and families can pick up a set of laminated “Youth Observation Cards” that guide them through simple activities: counting the number of different bee species at a flower patch, drawing a hummingbird’s feeding pattern, or identifying a night-blooming flower’s scent. Completed cards can be returned at the garden’s visitor center for a small plantable seed packet.

Starting July 12, a volunteer-led weekend walk program will operate every Saturday and Sunday morning from 10 a.m. to 11:30 a.m. Trained docents will lead groups along the path, offering deeper explanations of plant-pollinator relationships, habitat requirements, and seasonal changes. The walks are free with garden admission and do not require advance registration.

The Pollinator Path is the result of a year-long planning process that included input from garden horticulturists, educators, and accessibility consultants. It aligns with the garden’s broader mission to provide environmental education without political advocacy. The trail does not address pesticide policy, land-use regulation, or agricultural practices; instead, it focuses solely on botanical and ecological information that individuals can apply at home.

Harborleaf Botanical Garden, founded in 1972, maintains 35 acres of curated landscapes, including a woodland garden, desert collection, and seasonal bloom displays. It hosts approximately 150,000 visitors annually and offers year-round educational programs for all ages. The east meadow loop previously served as a seasonal wildflower viewing area; the new trail repurposes and enhances that space with permanent interpretive features.

The Pollinator Path officially opens on July 12, 2026, with a brief ribbon-cutting ceremony at 9:30 a.m. at the east meadow loop entrance. The garden opens to the public at 10 a.m. that day. Admission prices remain unchanged: $12 for adults, $8 for seniors and students, and free for children under 5. Garden members enter free year-round.

For more information about the Pollinator Path, including a detailed trail map, seasonal bloom calendars, and volunteer opportunities for the weekend walk program, visit the Harborleaf Botanical Garden website or contact the communications office.

**Media Contact:**
Jordan Chen
Communications Coordinator
Harborleaf Botanical Garden
(555) 123-4567, ext. 302
j.chen@harborleafgarden.org

# # #

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word non-erotic literary romance scene in English. Scenario: two former neighbors meet by chance in a small coastal laundromat during a power outage. Years ago, one of them left without saying goodbye. Tonight, a battery lantern, a half-finished load of towels, and an old voicemail become the center of the conversation.

Requirements:
- Focus on restrained emotional realism: regret, tenderness, defensiveness, and the difficulty of apologizing.
- Keep the entire piece to one scene.
- Do not use coercion, stalking, humiliation, explicit sexual content, or melodrama.
- Let physical details and subtext carry the emotion; dialogue should be sparse but meaningful.
- End with a quiet unresolved gesture rather than a full confession.

message.content:
The laundromat clung to the cliffside like a barnacle. Salt-eaten and fluorescent, it hummed a low, constant note against the crash of the Atlantic. Inside, the air was thick with the ghost-scent of detergent and damp cotton. The power had cut fifteen minutes ago, plunging the room into a grainy twilight, the only light now a single battery-powered lantern that Mina had found behind the change machine.

She set the lantern on the edge of a folding table. Its weak cone lit the floor, the base of the dryers, and the ankles of a man standing by the row of dark, dead washing machines.

She hadn’t seen him come in. The rain had been too loud. But she knew the shape of him—the way he held his shoulders, the precise stillness of a man who was listening.

“Luke,” she said. Not a question.

He turned. The lantern light carved half his face out of shadow: the line of his jaw, the slight frown that had always been his default expression. Older now. A few threads of grey at his temples. “Mina.”

Five years. He’d left a duplex in the middle of a Tuesday. She’d come home to a clean, empty apartment and a key on the kitchen counter. No note, no call. Just the silence of a door that had clicked shut.

She looked down at her own washer. Number four. It was half-full of towels—good, heavy bath towels she’d driven forty minutes to wash because the water at her rental was iron-stained. The cycle had been interrupted mid-agitation. The towels sat in a gray, tepid puddle.

“The outage,” he said. His voice was rougher than she remembered. “Came from the substation on Harbor Road. They’re saying maybe an hour.”

“I saw the truck.” She touched the cool glass of the washer door. “I guess my towels wait.”

A silence settled between them, thick as the salt air. The storm outside pressed against the windows. She could hear the drag and hiss of waves eating the shore.

He moved closer, not to her, but to the table where the lantern sat. He set a small, leather-bound notebook on the metal surface. “I still have something of yours.”

She looked at the notebook. It was hers. A pale blue Moleskine she’d kept in her nightstand drawer, full of lists and fragments of poetry she’d never shown anyone. She’d assumed it was lost in the move.

“You took it,” she said. It wasn’t an accusation. It was a fact, offered carefully.

He shook his head. “You left it in the yard. The day before I… left. I found it in the grass. I meant to give it back.” He paused. “And then I didn’t.”

The weight of that—*the day before I left*—hung in the air. She could see it now: the overgrown yard, the rusted gate, the late summer heat. They’d argued about something small. Who would buy the next bag of coffee. A missing trash bin. It hadn’t mattered. What had mattered was the way he’d looked at her afterward, like he was already gone.

She opened the notebook. The pages were dry, the ink faded. She turned to a page near the middle and found a single line she’d written in a hurry: *“The sea forgets its own salt.”*

She closed it. “Why didn’t you say goodbye?”

The question came out flat, without demand. She’d practiced it in her head a thousand times—different versions, with anger, with tears. But in the thin lantern light, with the rain and the waves for company, she had no energy for performance.

Luke ran a hand over his face. In the shadow, his expression was hard to read. “Because I didn’t know how,” he said. “I thought if I said it, I wouldn’t be able to go. And I had to go.”

“For what?”

“Work. A job in Portland. I told myself it was that. But it was easier to leave than to stay and keep failing.”

“Failing at what?”

He looked at her then, directly. The lantern made his eyes look dark and deep. “At being someone you could count on.”

Mina pressed her palm flat against the cold washer lid. “I never asked you to be someone else.”

“I know.” His voice was low. “That was the worst part.”

The wind shook the door. Somewhere down the coast, a ship’s horn sounded, low and mournful. She thought about the voicemail. She’d kept it for three years before she finally deleted it. A message he’d left on her phone the night he left, which she hadn’t listened to until the next morning. His voice had been rough, uncertain. *“Mina. I’m sorry I didn’t say it in person. I just… I don’t know how to do this. I don’t know how to be what you need. I’ll call when I get there.”*

He’d never called.

“Do you still have the same number?” she asked.

He blinked. “Yes. Why?”

She took a breath. The air tasted of salt and rust and the faint bite of the lantern’s battery. “Because I have a voicemail I never returned.”

His face changed. The defensiveness softened, replaced by something raw and exposed. “I didn’t deserve a return call.”

“That’s not why I didn’t call back.” She leaned against the machine. The metal was cool through her thin sweater. “I didn’t call back because I was afraid that if I heard your voice again, I wouldn’t be able to stay angry. And I needed to be angry. It was the only thing that made the empty apartment bearable.”

He said nothing. The silence was not empty. It was full of five years of separate tides, separate storms.

The lights flickered once, twice, then held. The fluorescent bars hummed back to life, and the laundromat snapped into harsh clarity: the linoleum floor, the coin machine, the posters for lost cats and yoga classes. The lantern seemed small and useless now.

Machine number four beeped, its cycle interrupted. The water inside sloshed.

Luke picked up the notebook and held it out to her. She took it. His fingers brushed hers for a half-second—dry, cool, and then gone.

“I’m sorry,” he said.

The words were simple. They didn’t explain or excuse. They sat between them like a stone on a beach, wet and ordinary.

She looked at the notebook in her hands, then at the dark window where the rain had begun to ease. “I know.”

She slid the notebook into her bag and walked to the door. The handle was cold. She pushed it open, and the night air rushed in, salty and clean.

Behind her, she heard him say, “Mina.”

She stopped. Didn’t turn.

“I’m staying at the Blue Gull. Room 7. For another week.”

The rain had stopped. The clouds were breaking, and a sliver of moon showed through, silvering the wet pavement. She stood in the doorway, half in the light of the laundromat, half in the dark of the coast.

“The towels,” she said, her voice quiet, “are still wet.”

Then she stepped out into the night, leaving the door open behind her. The wind caught it, held it, as if waiting to see if he would follow.

messages.content:
Write an 800–1,000 word technical article for backend engineers titled by you. Topic: designing rate limiting for a multi-tenant API used by small teams and enterprise customers.

You must cover:
1. The difference between authentication, authorization, quotas, and rate limits.
2. Token bucket vs. fixed window vs. sliding window at a practical level.
3. How to avoid punishing an entire tenant for one noisy integration.
4. What headers and error messages should be returned to API clients.
5. Observability: metrics, logs, and alerts that show whether limits are protecting reliability without blocking legitimate use.

Constraints: no code; no vendor-specific services; include a short “recommended baseline design” section at the end.

message.content:
# Designing Rate Limiting for a Multi-Tenant API: Balancing Protection with Flexibility

Rate limiting is one of those infrastructure decisions that seems simple at first but quickly unravels into a tangle of trade-offs when you support both a 5-person startup and a Fortune 500 enterprise on the same API. Getting it wrong means either frustrating your most valuable customers or letting a noisy neighbor degrade reliability for everyone. This article walks through the engineering decisions behind a rate-limiting system that serves both small teams and enterprise tenants fairly.

## 1. Foundational Distinctions: Auth, Authorization, Quotas, and Rate Limits

Before designing, we need clarity on four concepts that engineers often conflate.

**Authentication** answers “Who are you?” It validates credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens, JWT) and establishes identity. It does not control usage.

**Authorization** answers “What are you allowed to do?” It checks permissions—can this tenant call the `POST /admin/billing` endpoint? It is about access rights, not request frequency.

**Quotas** answer “How much total usage are you entitled to over a billing period?” A quota might say 1,000,000 requests per month. Quotas are enforced at the end of a period (or proactively with soft limits), typically for billing and planning.

**Rate limits** answer “How fast can you send requests right now?” They protect system stability by preventing short-term bursts from overwhelming resources. Rate limits operate on sub-second to minute windows. They are the first line of defense, whereas quotas are the accounting layer.

In a multi-tenant system, you must apply these independently. A tenant might have a high quota (enterprise contract) but still need per-second rate limits to prevent a misbehaving integration from starving other tenants.

## 2. Token Bucket vs. Fixed Window vs. Sliding Window: Practical Trade-offs

Three common algorithms exist, and each has operational implications for multi-tenant fairness.

**Fixed Window** increments a counter per time window (e.g., 100 requests per minute, resetting at :00). It is simple to implement in Redis with `INCR` and `EXPIRE`. The critical flaw: traffic spikes at the boundary. If a tenant sends 100 requests at :59 and 100 more at :01, they effectively achieve 200 requests in two seconds. This violates the “rate” guarantee and punishes other tenants competing for the same resources.

**Sliding Window Log** tracks timestamps of each request and counts those within the window. It is accurate but memory-intensive for high-throughput tenants (requires storing potentially thousands of timestamps). For enterprise tenants with millions of requests, this becomes expensive.

**Sliding Window Counter** (e.g., using Redis sorted sets or rolling counters) keeps a few sub-windows (e.g., 6 buckets per minute) and estimates the count. It achieves near-fixed-window simplicity with much better boundary behavior. Most practical implementations use this: store two buckets (current and previous) and interpolate. The trade-off is slight approximation error (1–2%), which is acceptable for rate limiting but not for billing.

**Token Bucket** allows bursts up to a configured capacity (bucket size) while limiting the sustained rate (refill rate). A tenant with a 1,000 requests/second token bucket and a bucket size of 2,000 can burst 2,000 requests if idle, then settle to 1,000/s. This is ideal for enterprise customers with legitimate batch operations (e.g., syncing large datasets at midnight). The downside: it requires tracking token state per tenant (e.g., last refill timestamp), which adds storage cost but avoids the clock-alignment issues of window algorithms.

For a multi-tenant system, I recommend **token bucket per tenant** for the core limit, combined with a **sliding window counter** for hard global limits. Token bucket gives tenants predictable burst behavior. The sliding window counter acts as a safety net for the entire infrastructure.

## 3. Protecting Other Tenants from a Noisy Integration

The hardest problem in multi-tenant rate limiting: one enterprise tenant’s misconfigured integration (e.g., a retry loop gone wild) should not degrade another tenant’s latency or error rate. Three techniques work together.

**Isolation by enforcement point.** Rate-limit decisions must be made per tenant, not globally. Use the tenant ID (extracted from the API key or JWT claim) as the key for your token bucket. Never apply a single global rate limit that all tenants share—that protects your infrastructure but punishes quiet tenants.

**Hierarchical rate limits.** Implement tiers: a per-endpoint rate limit, a per-tenant rate limit, and a global cluster-level limit. For example, the per-tenant token bucket allows 500 req/s. A separate global sliding window of 5,000 req/s across all tenants protects the database. If one tenant sends 3,000 req/s, the per-tenant bucket rejects the excess, but the global limit ensures the remaining two-thirds of capacity stays available for others.

**Concurrency-based backpressure.** Rate limits based on request rate alone miss the problem of long-running requests. A tenant might send 10 requests, each taking 30 seconds, consuming all database connection pool slots. Add a separate “concurrent requests” limiter (a semaphore per tenant) that caps parallel in-flight requests. This prevents one tenant from occupying all worker threads.

**Delayed rejection for enterprise customers.** For small teams, dropping excess requests with `429 Too Many Requests` is fine. For enterprise customers, consider an optional “slow queue” that delays requests beyond the limit instead of dropping them, with a small buffer. This avoids catastrophic retry storms. The queue must have bounded depth and a TTL—never unbounded.

## 4. Headers and Error Messages Clients Need

Clients cannot react to rate limits without clear feedback. The API must return these headers on every response (not just on 429):

- `X-RateLimit-Limit`: The maximum rate allowed for this tenant (e.g., “500” for requests/second)
- `X-RateLimit-Remaining`: How many requests remain in the current window/bucket before hitting the limit (e.g., “423”)
- `X-RateLimit-Reset`: Unix timestamp when the limit resets (or when the token bucket will refill to at least one request). This lets clients compute backoff.

On 429 responses, additionally return:

- `Retry-After`: Seconds the client should wait before retrying (integer). This is the most actionable header for simple retry logic.
- A JSON body:

```json
{
  "error": "rate_limit_exceeded",
  "message": "You have exceeded your API rate limit. Retry after 12 seconds.",
  "retry_after_seconds": 12,
  "limit": 500,
  "remaining": 0,
  "reset_at": 1712345678
}
```

The error code (`rate_limit_exceeded`) must be distinct from `quota_exceeded` (billing limit) and `insufficient_permissions` (authorization). Clients must never confuse these.

For multi-tenant notability: log the tenant ID and the violating integration’s IP or API key identifier in the error response (but never expose other tenants’ info). This helps clients debug and helps you identify noisy integrations in support tickets.

## 5. Observability: Are Limits Protecting Without Blocking?

Rate limiting can become a silent villain—blocking legitimate use while letting abuse through. Observability must answer both directions.

**Metrics to collect** (per tenant tag, per endpoint tag, per status code tag):

- `rate_limit.throttled_requests_total`: Count of requests that received 429. Split by tenant and endpoint.
- `rate_limit.requests_before_throttle`: Distribution (histogram) of how many requests tenants made before hitting the limit in a window. Spikes here indicate limits are too tight or tenants are misconfiguring.
- `rate_limit.token_bucket.remaining`: Track the current bucket level per tenant. If it stays near zero for long periods, the limit is too low. If it never drops below 90%, the limit is too generous.
- `api.latency_p99`: Grouped by tenant. If throttling prevents overload, latency should remain stable even as total request volume increases. If latency climbs despite low throttle counts, the limit is not protecting the bottleneck.
- `api.error_rate` (non-429): If error rates (5xx) drop after introducing rate limits, the system is working. If they rise, something is wrong (e.g., rate limiting causing thundering herd elsewhere).

**Logs**: Log every 429 with tenant ID, endpoint, HTTP method, and the client’s user-agent or integration identifier. Also log when a tenant crosses 80% of their limit (warning) and 100% (critical). This creates a trail for post-mortems when a customer complains about their requests being dropped.

**Alerts**: The most critical alert is not “high throttle rate” but **“throttle rate increasing while error rate (5xx) remains flat”** —that indicates limits are protecting resources. Conversely, **“throttle rate increasing AND error rate increasing”** means the limits are too late or too permissive. Additionally, alert on **“no throttling for any tenant for >24 hours”** —this likely means limits are set too high to ever trigger, providing no protection.

Set a P1 alert if global infrastructure metrics (CPU, connection pool utilization, DB query latency) exceed thresholds, even if throttle rates are low. That indicates you’ve misconfigured the limits to not activate early enough.

## 6. Recommended Baseline Design

For a team building a new multi-tenant API, start with this minimal but sound design:

- **Algorithm**: Token bucket per tenant (one bucket per tenant plus one per endpoint if needed). Store `last_refill_timestamp` and `tokens` in Redis with a TTL equal to the refill period (so idle tenants don’t waste memory).
- **Tiers**: Per-tenant limit (e.g., 200 req/s), per-tenant burst limit (e.g., 400), global sliding window counter (e.g., 5,000 req/s across all tenants). Apply the global limit after the per-tenant limit to catch aggregate abuse.
- **Concurrency**: Add a per-tenant semaphore with max_in_flight = 10. Implement in middleware, not in application code.
- **Headers**: Return `X-RateLimit-Limit`, `X-RateLimit-Remaining`, `X-RateLimit-Reset`, and `Retry-After` (on 429).
- **Error responses**: Use a consistent JSON schema with `error`, `message`, `retry_after_seconds`, `limit`, `remaining`, `reset_at`.
- **Observability**: Export the four metrics listed above (throttled requests, requests before throttle, bucket remaining, latency P99) with tenant and endpoint dimensions. Log every 429 and warning-level near-limit events.
- **Configuration**: Store rate limits in a dynamic config store (not hardcoded). Let enterprise support adjust per-tenant limits without a deploy.

Expand from this baseline: add per-endpoint limits for expensive endpoints (e.g., `/reports/generate`), add cost-weighted limits where one request may weigh more than one (e.g., 1 point for GET, 5 points for POST with large payload), and consider a “retry budget” for paying customers to auto-escalate limits during high-priority integrations.

Rate limiting done right feels invisible. Users should never think about it—but when a misconfiguration happens, your system should survive unscathed, and your logs should tell the story of exactly which tenant caused the pressure and how the limits held the line.

请帮我写一个单文件 HTML 水族箱动画。

要求：
1. 画面里有多条不同颜色、不同大小的鱼，游动要尽量自然。
2. 用户左键点击水族箱时，可以在点击位置放下一粒鱼食。
3. 每条鱼会追逐离自己最近的鱼食并尝试吃掉它；没有鱼食时恢复自由游动。
4. 需要有完整的 HTML、CSS 和 JavaScript，能直接保存为一个 `.html` 文件运行。
5. 视觉效果要精致一些，不要只给伪代码或解释，直接给出完整代码。

请帮我创建一个单文件 HTML 时钟应用，要求直接给出完整代码。

功能和视觉要求：
1. 时钟表盘是圆形，有刻度、数字、时针、分针、秒针，并在表盘内部显示当前时间和日期。
2. 表盘需要有简洁的高级感：白色背景、深色边框、轻微 3D 阴影。
3. 用 JavaScript 动态生成 60 个刻度，整点刻度更粗更深。
4. 时间必须转换为中国标准时间（北京时区，Asia/Shanghai）。
5. 写一个 updateClock() 函数，计算时针、分针、秒针角度；秒针需要尽量平滑运动。
6. 使用 setInterval 或 requestAnimationFrame 驱动刷新。
7. 代码需要兼容 Edge 浏览器。

请将下面的中文商务写作材料翻译成英文。要求：面向现代职场读者，语气清楚、专业、克制；不要翻译成生硬腔调；保留作者关于“商务信函应传达信息并建立善意”的核心观点。

素材来源：根据公共领域英文书《The Book of Business Etiquette》相关章节改写的中文素材
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23025
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists the source eBook as public domain in the USA; this Chinese passage is a benchmark rewrite.

【待处理素材】
许多商务信函本不必写，而已经写出的信中，又有不少因为信息残缺、结构混乱，使原本两封信便能完成并归档的交易，拖成六封或八封往返。好的信件来自清楚的思考和周密的安排。销售函有时需要数周打磨；普通业务往来只需几分钟也足够。关键不在于花了多久，而在于最终写出的信是否能准确完成任务。

衡量商务信函的规则很多，但真正不可缺少的只有两点：第一，信必须让收件人明白你的意思；第二，它必须为公司建立善意。最好的信，是用最简短、最礼貌、最完整的方式做到这两点。每封信背后都应有明确理由，哪怕只是向客户说一声谢谢。许多精力浪费在无目的的广告、无效的推销和多余的来信上；这些文字占用了打字机和纸张，却没有推动任何事情前进。

一封好信不应让读者注意它本身的文采，而应让读者接收到它要传达的消息。某出版社曾寄出一批推销新书的测试信，回信者称赞信写得漂亮，却无人订购图书。这说明文字若抢走了信息的焦点，再优雅也不是成功的商务写作。信纸、信头、版式、段落都应整洁、克制、清楚；过分花哨的设计和故作新奇的格式，只会让人分心。

商务写作还应尊重读者的时间。清楚的标题、适当的称呼、按主题分段的正文、明确的请求和完整的联系方式，往往比华丽措辞更能建立信任。若一封信需要收件人回复，写信者应说明希望对方在何时以前回复、需要提供哪些信息，以及下一步将如何处理。这样，信件不仅传递态度，也推动事务向前。

message.content:
Many business letters should never have been written in the first place. And among those that are written, a significant number suffer from missing information or poor structure, turning what could have been settled in two letters into a drawn-out exchange of six or eight. Good letters come from clear thinking and careful planning. While a sales letter may take weeks to refine, a routine business communication can be completed in a few minutes. What matters is not how long it takes, but whether the final letter does its job accurately.

There are many rules for evaluating business correspondence, but only two are truly essential. First, the letter must make your meaning clear to the reader. Second, it must build goodwill for the company. The best letters accomplish both goals in the shortest, most courteous, and most complete manner possible. Every letter should have a clear reason behind it, even if it is simply to say thank you to a customer. Too much effort is wasted on aimless advertisements, ineffective pitches, and unnecessary correspondence—words that consume paper and typing time without moving anything forward.

A well-written letter should not draw attention to its own style. Instead, it should let the message come through clearly. A publishing house once sent out a batch of test letters promoting a new book. Many recipients wrote back praising the letter’s elegance, but no one placed an order. This shows that if the writing overshadows the message, even the most polished language is a failure in business communication. Letterhead, layout, and paragraph structure should all be clean, restrained, and clear. Overly elaborate design or forced novelty only distracts the reader.

Business writing should also respect the reader’s time. A clear subject line, proper salutation, well-organized paragraphs by topic, a specific request, and complete contact details build trust far more effectively than ornate phrasing. If a letter requires a response, the writer should state the expected deadline, the information needed, and what will happen next. In this way, the letter not only conveys an attitude but also moves the matter forward.

messages.content:
请将下面的繁体中文寓言/哲理片段翻译成英文，目标读者为青少年读者。要求：保留想象力和节奏；遇到典故不要长篇解释；译文应清楚、流畅，适合作为儿童文学或寓言文本阅读。

素材来源：《莊子的故事》节选：逍遥游开篇
来源链接：https://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/23913
版权/授权说明：Project Gutenberg lists this eBook as public domain in the USA.

【待处理素材】
北冥有魚，其名為鯤。鯤之大，不知其幾千里也。化而為鳥，其名為鵬。鵬之背，不知其幾千里也；怒而飛，其翼若垂天之雲。是鳥也，海運則將徙於南冥。南冥者，天池也。齊諧者，志怪者也。諧之言曰：「鵬之徙於南冥也，水擊三千里，摶扶搖而上者九萬里，去以六月息者也。」野馬也，塵埃也，生物之以息相吹也。天之蒼蒼，其正色邪？其遠而無所至極邪？其視下也，亦若是則已矣。且夫水之積也不厚，則其負大舟也無力。覆杯水於坳堂之上，則芥為之舟；置杯焉則膠，水淺而舟大也。風之積也不厚，則其負大翼也無力。故九萬里，則風斯在下矣，而後乃今掊風；背負青天而莫之夭閼者，而後乃今將圖南。蜩與學鳩笑之曰：「我決起而飛，槍榆枋而止，時則不至而控於地而已矣，奚以這九萬里而南為？」適莽蒼者，三餐而反，腹猶果然；適百里者，宿舂糧；適千里者，三月聚糧。之二蟲又何知！小知不及大知，小年不及大年。奚以知其然也？朝菌不知晦朔，蟪蛄不知春秋，此小年也。楚之南有冥靈者，以五百歲為春，五百歲為秋；上古有大椿者，以八千歲為春，八千歲為秋。此大年也。而彭祖乃今以久特聞，眾人匹之，不亦悲乎！湯之問棘也是已。窮髮之北有冥海者，天池也。有魚焉，其廣數千里，未有知其脩者，其名為鯤。有鳥焉，其名為鵬，背若泰山，翼若垂天之雲，摶扶搖羊角而上者九萬里，絕雲氣，負青天，然後圖南，且適南冥也。斥鴳笑之曰：「彼且奚適也？我騰躍而上，不過數仞而下，翱翔蓬蒿之間，此亦飛之至也，而彼且奚適也？」此小大之辯也。故夫知效一官，行比一鄉，德合一君，而徵一國者，其自視也亦若此矣。而宋榮子猶然笑之。且舉世而譽之而不加勸，舉世而非之而不加沮，定乎內外之分，辯乎榮辱之竟，斯已矣。彼其於世，未數數然也。雖然，猶有未樹也。夫列子御風而行，泠然善也，旬有五日而反。彼於致福者，未數數然也。此雖免乎行，猶有所待者也。若夫乘天地之正，而御六氣之辯，以遊無窮者，彼且惡乎待哉！故曰：至人無己，神人無功，聖人無名。

message.content:
**The Tale of Kun and Peng**  

In the dark Northern Sea, there lived a fish named Kun.  
How big was Kun?  
No one truly knows—it stretched across thousands of miles.  

One day, this immense fish transformed into a bird.  
Its name was Peng.  
And the back of Peng?  
It, too, stretched across thousands of miles.  

When Peng rose in fury and took flight,  
its wings spread like clouds hanging from the edge of the sky.  

This bird would ride the ocean currents and migrate to the Southern Sea—  
the Heavenly Pool.  

There is an ancient book called *Qixie*, which collects tales of wonders.  
It says:  
> “When Peng journeys to the Southern Sea,  
> it stirs the waters for three thousand miles.  
> It spirals up on a whirlwind to a height of ninety thousand miles,  
> and sets off on the sixth-month gale.”  

Like wild horses galloping across the plains,  
like dust swirling in the air,  
all living things rise and fall with the breath of the world.  

The sky above—deep, endless blue—  
is that its true color?  
Or is it so far away that we can never reach its end?  
And when Peng looks down from above,  
does it see the same endless blue?  

If water is not deep enough,  
it cannot carry a great boat.  
Pour a cup of water into a hollow in the floor,  
and a mustard seed can sail on it like a boat.  
But place a cup there, and it sticks—  
because the water is shallow and the “boat” is too big.  

If wind is not thick enough,  
it cannot support great wings.  

That’s why, only when the wind gathers for ninety thousand miles,  
can Peng ride it—its back against the blue sky,  
nothing blocking its way—  
and then set out for the south.  

The cicada and the little dove laughed at Peng.  
“We fly up with all our might,” they chirped,  
“and land in the elm or the rosebush.  
Sometimes we don’t even make it—we just fall to the ground.  
What’s the point of flying ninety thousand miles to the south?”  

If you’re going to the countryside just outside town,  
you take three meals and come back, still full.  
If you’re traveling a hundred miles,  
you grind grain the night before.  
If you’re going a thousand miles,  
you stock up food for three months.  

What do those two little creatures know?  

Small understanding cannot grasp great understanding.  
Short-lived years cannot comprehend long-lived ones.  

How do we know this?  
A morning mushroom knows nothing of dusk and dawn.  
A summer cicada knows nothing of spring and autumn.  
These are short-lived.  

South of Chu, there is a sacred tree called Mingling—  
it counts five hundred years as one spring,  
and another five hundred years as one autumn.  

In ancient times, there was a giant tree called Dachun—  
it counted eight thousand years as one spring,  
and another eight thousand years as one autumn.  
These are long-lived.  

Yet humans today only talk about Pengzu, who lived eight hundred years—  
and they compare themselves to him.  
Isn’t that sad?  

King Tang of Shang once asked his wise minister Ji about this.  
In the barren north, there is a dark sea—the Heavenly Pool.  
In it lives a fish, thousands of miles wide—  
no one knows how long.  
Its name is Kun.  

There also lives a bird named Peng.  
Its back is like Mount Tai.  
Its wings are like clouds hanging from the sky.  
It spirals up on a whirlwind, rising ninety thousand miles,  
piercing through clouds and mist,  
its back against the blue sky—  
then it turns south and heads for the Southern Sea.  

A little quail laughed at it.  
“Where does it think it’s going?  
I leap up, fly a few feet, then land again.  
I dart among the bushes and weeds—  
that’s the best kind of flying!  
Where does it think it’s going?”  

This is the difference between small and great.  

There are people whose wisdom fits a single office,  
whose conduct suits a single village,  
whose virtue pleases a single ruler,  
whose talent wins the trust of a whole kingdom.  
They look at themselves the same way the little quail does.  

Song Rongzi would only laugh at them.  

Even if the whole world praised him, he wouldn’t be encouraged.  
Even if the whole world blamed him, he wouldn’t be discouraged.  
He knew clearly the line between inner and outer,  
between honor and disgrace.  
That was all.  

But even he had not yet found his true foundation.  

Master Liezi could ride the wind,  
light and swift,  
and return after fifteen days.  
He was not obsessed with seeking blessings.  
Yet even he still depended on something—the wind.  

But what of one who rides the natural way of Heaven and Earth,  
who masters the changes of the six energies,  
and wanders through the infinite?  
What would such a one need to depend on?  

That is why it is said:  
The Perfect Person has no self.  
The Spirit Person has no achievements.  
The Sage has no name.
